tables.name: RequestLog
tables.description: Permanent audit log of all Jira API interactions including successful ticket creations, failures, and queue insert errors.
tables.dataFlow: Process-JiraTicketQueue.ps1 inserts one row per Jira API interaction (both successes and failures). sp_QueueTicket also writes directly to RequestLog when a queue INSERT fails (RequestType = 'QueueInsertFailed', StatusCode = -99). Calling modules query this table for deduplication before queuing new tickets, checking Trigger_Type and Trigger_Value with a successful StatusCode to avoid creating duplicate Jira tickets for the same condition.

columns.name: LogID
columns.description: Unique identifier for this log entry

columns.name: SourceModule
columns.description: Module that originated the request (e.g., JobFlow, NoticeRecon)

columns.name: RequestType
columns.description: Type of request: CreateTicket, QueueInsertFailed, etc

columns.name: TicketKey
columns.description: Created ticket key (e.g., SD-12345). NULL if failed. 'Email' if fallback used

columns.name: ResponseMessage
columns.description: Full API response or error message

columns.name: CreatedDate
columns.description: When this log entry was created

columns.name: CreatedBy
columns.description: Process or user that created this log entry

columns.name: Trigger_Type
columns.description: Category for deduplication lookup (e.g., JobFlow_Stall, DM_Template)

designNotes.note: Rows are never updated or deleted. Each API interaction generates a separate entry for complete ticket creation history.

sp_QueueTicket catches INSERT failures and logs them directly to RequestLog with StatusCode = -99 and RequestType = 'QueueInsertFailed'. This ensures visibility even when the queue INSERT itself fails.

Same pattern as Teams: calling modules query RequestLog before queuing tickets to check if a successful creation already exists for the same Trigger_Type and Trigger_Value combination. Prevents duplicate Jira tickets across orchestrator cycles.

statusValues.meaning: Bad Request — check custom field IDs and required fields
Forbidden — check user permissions in Jira project
Not Found — verify ProjectKey and IssueType exist in Jira
Server Error — Jira-side issue, retry later
Queue insert failed — check sp_QueueTicket error in ResponseMessage
Ticket created successfully in Jira — value is the actual Jira ticket key
Fallback email sent instead of ticket — max retries exhausted
API call failed — no ticket or email created

queries.name: Recent activity
queries.description: Last 20 API interactions showing source module, ticket key, status code, and timestamp.
queries.sql:
SELECT TOP 20
    SourceModule,
    RequestType,
    ProjectKey,
    Summary,
    TicketKey,
    StatusCode,
    CreatedDate
FROM Jira.RequestLog
ORDER BY CreatedDate DESC;

queries.name: Failed API calls
queries.description: All entries with non-success status codes for troubleshooting.
queries.sql:
SELECT 
    LogID,
    SourceModule,
    RequestType,
    ProjectKey,
    Summary,
    StatusCode,
    ResponseMessage,
    CreatedDate
FROM Jira.RequestLog
WHERE StatusCode NOT IN (200, 201) OR StatusCode IS NULL
ORDER BY CreatedDate DESC;

queries.name: Volume by module
queries.description: Thirty-day ticket creation counts with success/failure breakdown per source module.
queries.sql:
SELECT 
    SourceModule,
    COUNT(*) AS total_requests,
    SUM(CASE WHEN StatusCode IN (200, 201) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS successful,
    SUM(CASE WHEN StatusCode NOT IN (200, 201) OR StatusCode IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS failed
FROM Jira.RequestLog
WHERE CreatedDate >= DATEADD(DAY, -30, GETDATE())
GROUP BY SourceModule
ORDER BY total_requests DESC;

queries.name: Standard deduplication pattern
queries.description: Calling modules should check this table before queuing to prevent duplicate tickets
queries.sql:
IF NOT EXISTS (
    SELECT 1 FROM Jira.RequestLog
    WHERE Trigger_Type = @TriggerType
      AND Trigger_Value = @TriggerValue
      AND StatusCode = 201
      AND TicketKey IS NOT NULL
      AND TicketKey != 'Email'
)
BEGIN
    EXEC Jira.sp_QueueTicket ...
END

relationshipNotes.note: QueueID links log entries back to the original queue row. No FK constraint exists but the column is present for tracing. Multiple RequestLog rows may reference the same QueueID when retries generate additional API calls.

tables.name: TicketQueue
tables.description: Queue table for pending ticket requests awaiting PowerShell processing. Records remain after processing for audit and troubleshooting.
tables.dataFlow: Tickets enter via Jira.sp_QueueTicket (from T-SQL callers) or direct INSERT. TR_Jira_TicketQueue_QueueDepth fires on INSERT, incrementing running_count in Orchestrator.ProcessRegistry to signal the orchestrator. Process-JiraTicketQueue.ps1 claims Pending rows (TicketStatus = 'Pending' AND RetryCount < MaxRetries), retrieves Jira credentials from dbo.Credentials via two-tier passphrase decryption (master passphrase from GlobalConfig), creates tickets via Jira REST API, and updates TicketStatus to Success or Failed with the returned TicketKey. On success the script performs a GET to retrieve the assigned user. Each API interaction is logged to Jira.RequestLog. When max retries are exhausted and EmailRecipients is populated, the script sends a fallback email via Database Mail and sets TicketStatus to EmailSent.

columns.name: QueueID
columns.description: Unique identifier for this queue entry

columns.name: SourceModule
columns.description: Module that queued the ticket (e.g., JobFlow, NoticeRecon)

columns.name: IssueType
columns.description: Jira issue type: Task, Issue, Bug, etc

columns.name: TicketPriority
columns.description: Priority: Highest, High, Medium, Low, Lowest

columns.name: Assignee
columns.description: Jira username to assign ticket to

columns.name: CascadingField_ID
columns.description: Field ID for cascading select (e.g., customfield_18401)

columns.name: TriggerValue
columns.description: Specific value for deduplication (e.g., date, template code)

columns.name: EmailRecipients
columns.description: Semicolon-separated email addresses for fallback if API fails

columns.name: TicketKey
columns.description: Jira ticket key if created (e.g., SD-12345)

columns.name: StatusCode
columns.description: HTTP status code from Jira API (201 = success)

designNotes.note: Same pattern as Teams: calling modules insert and continue without waiting for Jira API responses. Ticket creation latency does not affect the calling process execution time.

When Jira API calls fail after all retry attempts, the script sends a fallback email via Database Mail containing the ticket details for manual creation. Requires EmailRecipients to be populated on the queue row. TicketStatus is set to EmailSent as a terminal status.

Up to three arbitrary Jira custom fields plus one cascading select field can be specified per ticket. Field IDs (e.g., customfield_18401) and values are stored in the queue and mapped into the Jira API payload at creation time. This avoids hardcoding Jira field schemas into the table structure.

After successful ticket creation, the script performs a separate GET request to retrieve the assignee display name from Jira. This captures the actual assignee (which may differ from the requested assignee due to Jira automation rules) and logs it to RequestLog.

Uses System.Net.HttpWebRequest instead of Invoke-RestMethod because Jira returns WWW-Authenticate: Negotiate, causing PowerShell to attempt Windows integrated auth instead of honoring the Basic auth header.

statusValues.meaning: Waiting for processor pickup. Set on INSERT as the default value.
Most urgent tickets requiring immediate attention.
Default priority set by sp_QueueTicket. Used for most automated ticket creation.
Jira ticket created successfully. TicketKey populated with the Jira issue key (e.g., SD-12345).
Jira API call failed. RetryCount incremented, ResponseMessage contains error details. Failed rows are not automatically re-picked up since the pending query filters on TicketStatus = 'Pending'.
Max retries exhausted and fallback email sent via Database Mail. Terminal status indicating the ticket must be created manually from the email.

queries.name: Pending tickets
queries.description: Shows tickets awaiting processing with wait time, source module, and retry count.
queries.sql:
SELECT 
    QueueID,
    SourceModule,
    ProjectKey,
    Summary,
    TicketPriority,
    RetryCount,
    RequestedDate,
    DATEDIFF(MINUTE, RequestedDate, GETDATE()) AS minutes_waiting
FROM Jira.TicketQueue
WHERE TicketStatus = 'Pending'
ORDER BY RequestedDate;

queries.name: Failed tickets
queries.description: Shows failed tickets with error details and retry counts for troubleshooting.
queries.sql:
SELECT 
    QueueID,
    SourceModule,
    ProjectKey,
    Summary,
    TicketStatus,
    RetryCount,
    StatusCode,
    ResponseMessage,
    RequestedDate,
    ProcessedDate
FROM Jira.TicketQueue
WHERE TicketStatus IN ('Failed', 'EmailSent')
ORDER BY ProcessedDate DESC;

queries.name: Recent processing results
queries.description: Last 20 processed tickets showing source, status, ticket key, and timing.
queries.sql:
SELECT TOP 20
    QueueID,
    SourceModule,
    Summary,
    TicketStatus,
    TicketKey,
    RequestedDate,
    ProcessedDate
FROM Jira.TicketQueue
WHERE ProcessedDate IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY ProcessedDate DESC;

relationshipNotes.note: Each API interaction (success or failure) creates a RequestLog entry. No FK constraint but QueueID is stored in RequestLog for tracing back to the original queue row.
TR_Jira_TicketQueue_QueueDepth increments running_count on INSERT, signaling the orchestrator engine to launch Process-JiraTicketQueue.ps1 on its next heartbeat.
The processor retrieves Jira API credentials (URL, username, password) from dbo.Credentials using two-tier passphrase decryption. The master passphrase is stored in dbo.GlobalConfig.

procedures.name: sp_QueueTicket
procedures.description: Queue procedure that inserts Jira ticket requests into TicketQueue for asynchronous processing by the PowerShell processor.
procedures.dataFlow: Called by T-SQL modules to queue Jira ticket requests. Inserts one row into Jira.TicketQueue with Pending status. The INSERT triggers TR_Jira_TicketQueue_QueueDepth which signals the orchestrator to launch the processor. On INSERT failure, catches the error and logs directly to Jira.RequestLog with StatusCode = -99 and RequestType = 'QueueInsertFailed'.

designNotes.note: Wraps the queue INSERT in TRY/CATCH. On failure, logs the error to Jira.RequestLog so there is always a trace of the attempt even when the queue INSERT itself fails. The CATCH block has its own nested TRY/CATCH to handle the unlikely case where the RequestLog INSERT also fails.

Accepts up to three custom fields and one cascading select field via ID/value parameter pairs. This enables callers to target any Jira custom field by ID without requiring schema changes to the queue table when new Jira fields are introduced.

relationshipNotes.note: Primary INSERT target. This is the main T-SQL entry point for queuing Jira tickets.
Fallback write target on INSERT failure. Logs queue failures directly with StatusCode = -99 so failed attempts are always visible.

triggers.name: TR_Jira_TicketQueue_QueueDepth
triggers.description: INSERT trigger on Jira.TicketQueue that signals the Orchestrator v2 engine when tickets are ready for processing. Enables queue-driven execution of Process-JiraTicketQueue.

designNotes.note: INSERT trigger that increments running_count in Orchestrator.ProcessRegistry for Process-JiraTicketQueue. Only updates rows where run_mode = 2 (queue-driven), enabling the orchestrator to launch the processor on-demand rather than on a fixed polling schedule. Counts the number of inserted rows to accurately track queue depth across multi-row inserts.

relationshipNotes.note: Directly updates ProcessRegistry.running_count to signal queue depth. The orchestrator engine checks running_count on each heartbeat and launches the processor when count > 0.

scripts.name: Process-JiraTicketQueue.ps1
scripts.description: Queue processor that creates Jira tickets via REST API. Claims Pending tickets from TicketQueue, authenticates using encrypted credentials from dbo.Credentials, creates tickets in Jira, retrieves assignee information, updates queue status, and logs results to RequestLog. Sends email fallback via Database Mail when API calls fail after all retry attempts.
scripts.dataFlow: Launched by the orchestrator when TR_Jira_TicketQueue_QueueDepth signals queue depth > 0. Retrieves master passphrase from dbo.GlobalConfig, uses two-tier decryption against dbo.Credentials to obtain Jira URL, username, and password. Reads Pending tickets from Jira.TicketQueue (TicketStatus = 'Pending' AND RetryCount < MaxRetries), builds JSON payload mapping queue columns to Jira REST API fields (including custom fields and cascading selects), and creates tickets via POST to /rest/api/2/issue. On success, performs a GET to retrieve the actual assignee, updates TicketQueue with TicketKey and Success status, and logs to Jira.RequestLog. On failure, increments RetryCount and sets TicketStatus to Failed. When max retries are exhausted and EmailRecipients is populated, sends a fallback email via Database Mail and sets TicketStatus to EmailSent.

designNotes.note: Runs as a queue-driven process (run_mode = 2) in the orchestrator. Only launched when tickets are waiting, not on a fixed polling schedule.

Uses System.Net.HttpWebRequest instead of Invoke-RestMethod because Jira returns WWW-Authenticate: Negotiate in the response headers. PowerShell Invoke-RestMethod interprets this as a request for Windows integrated auth and attempts Negotiate authentication instead of using the explicitly provided Basic auth header. HttpWebRequest with PreAuthenticate = true forces Basic auth on the first request.

Credentials are stored encrypted in dbo.Credentials. The master passphrase (from GlobalConfig) decrypts a service-specific passphrase, which in turn decrypts the actual Jira URL, username, and password. No secrets are stored in the script or on disk.

When Jira API calls fail after all retry attempts, the script can send a fallback email via Database Mail containing the ticket summary, description, and error details. This ensures the request is not silently lost even when the Jira API is unreachable. Requires EmailRecipients to be populated on the queue row.

Accepts TaskId and ProcessId parameters from the orchestrator engine. Calls Complete-OrchestratorTask on completion with status, duration, and a summary of processed/created/failed/email counts. Supports -Execute safeguard (preview mode by default).

relationshipNotes.note: Primary data source. Reads Pending rows, updates TicketStatus, TicketKey, StatusCode, ResponseMessage, RetryCount, ProcessedDate, and LastRetryDate after each API interaction.
Write target. Inserts one row per API interaction (success or failure) for complete audit trail.
Reads encrypted Jira API credentials (URL, username, password) using two-tier passphrase decryption.
Reads master passphrase for credential decryption. Also used for retry configuration.
